CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Peelers and Poverty are leading lines in backward
parts of Treland. We hear that Ballinasloe is the worst
business town and probably the poorest in Ireland. It
is also, we are informed, a most shoneen and backbone-
i less place. Whether that description: of it is exag-
I gerated or not we cannot say; but if it is not the worst

5 business town we don’t suppose it is very far from it.

& 1t is in such unprogressive and backboneless places
that shoneenism spreads out large and peelers are of the
clite. This extraordinary disease of peeler-feasting is
an infallible sign of urban degenerncy—poor little
miserable hamlets reeking with lanes and poverty are
ever ready to find money to testimonial peelets. A
peeler by the name of O’Connor has been transferred
to Waterford, and Ballinasloe is all of a flutter, and a
~ meeting was held at a local bungery called ** The Im-

- the revival of the Irish language.

perial Hotel,” with a view to gefting up a testimonial
for the departed bobby. A Mr. Conroy, J.P., was
in the chair and spoke in very high terms of the police-
man; so also did one Davidson, a lawyer; Golding, a
Crown solicitor; and Rothwell, clerk to the Petty Ses-
sions—these we suppose are leaders of “‘ society ”’ in
Ballinasloe, where policemen are amongst the clite.
The meeting in the publichouse or hotel realised £10,
and there is to be another meeting. A local democratic
paper devotes a sort of editorial prose-poem to the
transferred bobby. We quote :—

Perhaps no member of the R.I.C. force of any
grade, high or low, enjoyed a greater or more de-
served popularity than he did during his long
period of service in Ballinasloe.

We do not think there is one in Ballinasloe to-
day who does not send a kind thought after him
to his new sphere of duty, mingled with the wish
that in it he may be as successful and as highly
esteemed as he here, and that merit may be
rewarded by his further promotion at no distant
date.

Waterford should welcome this great man with no-
thing less than a torchlight procession.

The National University is perhaps not good enough
for some of Ireland’s real—that is titled—aristocracy.
Sir Daniel Hegarty is one of Cork’s old nobility, his
title going ba s all Corkonians know, to the dim
and distant past when Boadicea visited Ireland.  Sir-
Daniel is a money lender—it is needless to specify upon
what he lends the money—as well as a noble knight,
and University College, Cork, a constituent college of
the National University, must be ‘“ no class’’ to such
as he. But why does the ‘“ Cork Examiner’’ head a
paragraph ‘‘ Success of a Cork Student,”” when the
paragraph merely announces that Master Hegarty, of
the noble line, passed the Junior Freshman examination
of the Parochial University in College Green? Where
does the “‘ success *’ worthy of a newspaper paragraph
come in? Are we to take it that it is an honour for a
Cork lad to pass such an examination! rerhaps the
Nationa! University matriculation examination is more
difficult > We believe the young Cork nobleman spent:
some of his earlier years in Clongowes |

The Dingle peninsula is one of the most Irish-speak-
ing parts of Ireland, but so far as local and other public
boards and bodies go they appear to be doing their best
to kill Irish there. Right officials of the Congested
Districts Board don’t know Irish; neither do the three
stewards of the Board employed in the district. These
are only a few of the facts. ‘‘A Kerryman,” in the
course of a letter to us says:—'° What in particular
strikes me in regard to the matter is the complete
apathy of the Kerry County Council on the subject of
With the exception
of passing a resolution in favour of compulsory Irish
in the National University, I don’t remember ever to
have seen any other action taken that would further
the intarests of, the language. They don’t seem to be
‘aware that even with Trish compulsory in the University
the fate of the language hinges more on what is done
in places like Dingle. Here we have yet in ene corner
of Ireland according to the last Census (1911) the large
number of 1,117 persons who know no English. Tf
Irish Had died out in the whole of the remainder of
TIreland we have here yet sufficient seed to restore it to

THE LEADER.

the whole country. The fact is the Kerry County
Council takes no interest in the Irish language, and
one never sees any initiative taken by that body that
would help the interests of Irish in Dingle or any other
‘part of the county. The County Council member for
Dingle, too, Mr. Baker, is a fluent Irish speaker, but
one never reads where he uses his influence at the
council meetings to draw attention to the advantages
of his district. ’

.. The same may be said of the other public boards
in Kerry: Urban Councils, ete.; one never sees where
they take any active measures to utilise the splendid
store of Irish in the county. Dingle or any other Irish-
speaking district in the county may be swamped with
English-speaking officials for all they know or care.”

Our correspondent also says:—‘‘ No wonder Irish is
not making the headway it should. The Gaelic League
branches are left to struggle on as best they can without
getting any assistance from the public bodies who could
soon make Irish a force if they exerted themselves in
the least. And what is to be said of the Dingle Con-
vent School with 475 girls on thie rolls, where no in-
struction in Irish is given in standards under the third ?
fﬂso the Christian Brothers, with 248 boys, where Trish
is taught in the sixth standard only ? Surely it is high
time that attention was drawn to these schools. Irish
is the language of 83 per cent. of the people of the dis-
trict, and a few half-hours weekly are given.to teaching
it in the schools named.””

Is_there no Irish speaking and Irish Ireland public
opinion in the Dingle district? Are the Irish speakers
so far enslaved that they have not enough grié even %o
protest ?

“ Anti-humbug >’ writes :—‘* Mr. William O’Brien’s
anxiety, as shown in his speech on the second reading
of the Home Rule Bill, to safeguard the innocent Pro-
testant lambs from the Papist wolves is amusing. Why,
the only people in Ireland who are at present in a tight
corner owing to the boycotting carried on by those same
gentle Protestant lambs are the Papists. A straw will
show how the wind blows. Lately a case came under
my notice for the authenticity of which I vouch. An
excellent Catholic cook, who has filled the very best
positions, was about to leave her place. She wrote
to a well-known lady in Armagh who keeps a registry
office for servants. She got a reply finding her a good
place at £35 a year with a North of Ireland lady. The
latter, however, on learning that the cook was a Catho-
lic, refused to take her. The cook was next put in
communication with two other ladies—one in Co. Car-
low, the other at Mitchelstown Castle.  Their first
question was what was her religion. On learning that
she was a Catholic they bluntly told her that they
would first try to get a Protestant cook. Failing that
one of them said she might engage her. The registry
lady in Armagh plainly told this Catholic woman that
there was no use in writing to her any more, that Pro-
testant ladies would not take Catholies at present. Com-
mend me to Mr. William O’Brien’s Protestant dames
for venomous and merciless boycotting of poor Catholic
servants. . This is the sort of thing that is rampant ab
present in Ireland the while William O’Brien and his
_merry men are preaching their tolerance rot from the
house tops and trying fo get the lambs of Armagh,
Derry, Down and Antrim more than their quota in the
representation of a Home Parliament. I vouch for the
accuracy of the facts here stated.”

THE LOST CAWS
(Sung by a Unionist).

On Ireland’s poor Union outlanders
The doom is descending, I fear,
Tor Englishmen tire of our slanders

And Iibels on Irishmen here.
They’re calling for ra jonal speeches,
Tor reasons and arguments cold,
Instead of foul ruffianly screeches

And calumnies centuries old.

Oh, how can poor fossils and fogeys
Long trained in ascendancy s school
Stand up without libels and bogies
To fight against Irish Home Rule.
Deprived of their bunkum and bluster
And threatened explosions of gas,
No reason at all can they muster
To show why the Bill shouldn’t pass.

Besides when they tall of construction
1t comes very silly and queer
Trom people whose aim is obstruction
Of all that the nation holds dear.
Whene’er in defence of their faction
They deal with statistics alone,

Like ‘98 yeomen in action, .
They injure no side but their own.

1 see not one sign of dissension
Throughout all the national hive,
Nor cranky discussion worth mention
The Unionist hopes to revive.
It seems not a one has a notion,

Not even friend William or Tim,
To cause a great shrieking commotion
In favour of Protestants grim.

' No hope can we build on Sinn Feiners,
Or Skefy’s henpecking brigade,
To help on poor Carson’s campaigners
gainst the Home Rulers arrayed.
Such faddists now cut a poor figure,
And very small trouble can brew,
Tor Ireland united in vigour

Cares not what the likes of them do.

The Union is near dissolution,
Let Carsonites realise that,
And all make a grim resolution
To practice the jump of the cat.
Here’s one, anyway, not a martyr,
But most economic and cool,
Who'll fight for a job like a tartar
When Ireland possesses Home Rule.
! A .M. W.

probable that Swift had some acquaintance with the

, and that it formed the basis of his  Voyage to

he has none.”” The italics are ours, and we are sure if
any but the English ‘ Times '’ made that outrageous

Where are all the critics of the Home Rule Bill?
The poor *‘ Irish Times ' has very little to say for
itself, though we had expected once the text of the Bill
was published that day by day it would be snarling
and barking at the details. The paper of Murphy
““ the Rebel ”’ is very quiet, too.  Surely such great
financiers as Mr. Murphy, and his man, Mr. Joe
Mooney. might be expected to be publishing columns
upon columns about the finance. So far, Mr. Ennis
appears to have been left almost a monopoly of that;
branch, and he does not appear to have added to his
reputation. It is curious, indeed, how some men
attain reputation. We Lknow very little of Mr. Ennis,
and probably we know as much of him as most people.
Until some time ago was a Wexford local public
man, and by some pro which we confess we can-

‘not trace, he quickly developed into being regarded as

one of our leading national experts on public finance.

THE LEADER.

295

We confess that we accepted him as such.. But now
when we ask ourselves upon what does his reputation
as o great public financier rest we are unable to supply
a satisfactory answer. His counting upon collected
revenue in Ireland as a stable foundation upon which to
erect anything does not increase our opinion of h1s\
judgment. Surely if a mere change in the method and *
place of paying certain taxes meant a large amount of
money going to the credit of Inglish revenue rather
than “to Irish revenue, it is to be expected that the
Tinglish people concerned would go to some inconveni-
ence with a view to getting the money to the credit
of their own country. 'Mr. Ennis strikes us as being
rather over-simple to sustain the character of astute
national financier.

What's up with Murphy’s ‘‘ Independent? ”’ No
one can prevent it from discussing the Bill. What
amendments has it to propose? Why does it not tell
its readers the financial arrangements that it has to
suggest as practical? Tt is easy for the ‘‘Independent”’
to be asking questions, and to state that it is the duty -
of Irishmen fo examine the Bill. But what concrete
suggestions has it to make? Tts leading article in
Tuesday’s issue throws very little light on the matter,
and we suspect the reason is that it has very little
light to throw. What is the use of it asking its readers
for instance—‘* Why should Ireland be deprived of the
right of collecting her own taxes? > Why does it not
turn round and answer the question? Does it think
that needy people pay their ha’pennies in order to have
State conundrums put before them? TFor our part we
would like to read real suggestions concerning. the
bettering of the Bill. But a junk of printed stuff
tailed off by the remark: ‘‘These are points which
deserve consideration *’ does not carry us very far.
If they deserve consideration, why does not the
‘“ha’penny dreadful ’  say something tangible and
definite and give its readers the benefit of its advice in
-part payment for their ha’pennies ?

So far as we can judge, the ‘ Independent’’ is
anxious to cover its poverty of resource in the way of
practical suggestion in face of a Bill that is too good to
attack by crying out like a small boy that it won’t be
allowed to speak its mind. Who is preventing it?
‘Who prevented it from publishing the silly letter from
the puppy who declared in its columns: ‘‘ Protestants,
as a body, do not study Irish; they never willlll"
Probably the fewer silly and cantankerous letters that
this “‘ rebel * paper publishes, and the less definite ex-
pressions of opinion that it makes on the Bill, the
better for its own reputation. And that fact may be
dawning upon it. Here is a choice sentence:—'‘ To
say that there should be no discussion of this Bill is
tantamount to saying that nothing more perfect was
ever framed.”” The *‘ dreadful ’ is badly off when it
has to pad an article with such ungrammatical stuff.
Of course, no one ever said anything of the sort in
grammatical or ungrammatical form. And the tame
paper that swallowed an apology because it was stated
by mistake that it put out a Union Jack need not think
that it can hide its poverty of suggestion by pretending
that there is a sort of conspiracy against discussion.

Last week we commented on the Armagh Catholic
Ladies’ Leap Year Ball, at which there was no Irish
dance, at which there was a “‘ twilight dance,’” and at
which the catering was done by the Grand Master of
the Armagh Orangemen. An Armagh correspondent,
writing to us, remarks :—‘‘ As you don’t seem to quite
understand what a ‘ twilight dance ’ is, I beg to inform
you for the information of your readers that a ¢ twilight
or sunset dance’ is a dance where the light is sud-
denly turned off, and the room left in absolute dark-
ness for a considerable time.”  Surely our correspon-
dent must be incorrect in stating that the ‘‘ twilight
dance ’’ involves absolute darkness, as the term ‘‘ twi-
light ** would seem to suggest at least some light. We
hear that several Nationalists were willing to tender
for the catering, but they were not asked to do so.
Owing to our drawing attention to this matter we
doubt if there will be any more ‘‘ twilight ” dances
under the auspices of Catholic ladies in the City
of St. Patrick; and the catering for the next dance
organised by Catholic ladies may possibly be thrown
open to competition.

T was almost exactly seven years to a day since we
had visited Carlow, and at that time the eyes of the
Irish industrial world were upon i, as it was the scens
of a brave attempt to establish a new industry that
would have to face the competition of two  hemi-
spheres. The story of the starting of the industry will
bear a brief ‘re-telling. . The LrabER industrial propa-
ganda had, if we may so express it, created a regular
scare,in favour of Irish industry. In 1903 some priests
and people in Carlow were caught by the industrial
microbe, and a public meeting was called. Such
meetings, if our memory serves us well, were fairly
frequent at that time, but how many of them resulted
in a verb “to do” we cannot say. If it is hard to
make people good by Act of Parliament, it is difficult
to found industries * in public meeting assembled.’’
In the Carlow case there was, fortunately, a man of
practical patriotism, able and willing to take up the
project single-handed; and Mv. Michael Governey,
though he then knew no more about boot-making than
we do, started the Carlow boot factory. We read with
a cerain nervousness of the startihg of new Irish indus-
tries, for we dread their failure, as every failure goes to
strengthen that depressing and native-killing modern
idea that ‘‘ nothing Irish succceds.’”” Seven years ago

We had known for some time that considerable ex-
tensions were being made, and we were anxious to pay
another visit to this concrete symbol of Irish industrial
reconstruction.  We certainly were not prepared for
the sight that met our eyes when we entered the
factory on Wednesday of last week. We should say
that it is about four or five times bigger than' the
factory we visited seven years agoj; it recalled at once
to our mind the Lee factory in Cork, though whether
it is as big or bigger we are not certain. It is all new
and modern, and it is interesting to note that it was
erected by a local firm to Mr. Governey’s own design.
The factory resembles the Central Hall of the Royal
Dublin Society premises at Ballsbridge; of course, it
is not as large, and the galleries that run completely
round it are comparatively wider. The factory
employs 150 hands, and the number will probably be
about 200 in a short time, yet the place shows no sign
of anything like congestion, and so far as space goes
there is still fair room for development within the
building. A great new gas engine supplies the power,
and a big'amount of power is required, as there are no
fewer than five lines of shafting working the multitude
of machines.

- It is interesting to look at the machines working,
but they are all beyond the comprehension of the mere
observing outsider. If you go through an up-to-date
boot factory your sense of wonder is excited by some
of the almost uncanny machines, but it would be use-
less to attempt to write about them. There they go,
pressing, cutting, stitching, hammering, and all the
rest of it, and the one impression you are likely to
take is, that if you would run a boot factory you would
need to commence early and keep your attention to
the business. That is just where the pluck of Mr.
Governey comes in, and where his enterprise is a hope-
ful headline to industrial Ireland. It was the Irish
industrial spirit and public spirit of Mr. Governey—
and as he was a reader of the LEaDER {rom
about the first number we may put in a little
claim to being responsible for some of his TIrish
industrial ardour—that induced him to take great risks
and attempt to establish an up-to-date boot factory in
a centre where everything had to‘start from the raw.
It is now about nine years since he started it, and
industrial Ireland will be glad to hear that the factory
is working at a profit. It is now an assured success,
and a productive industry employing close on 200
hands and expanding, is a big fact in a town like
Carlow, where ten years ago such an enterprise was
undreamf of. About six hands out of all those we see
busy as bees before us are imported, the rest were
trained in Carlow. It is a big achievement, and yet
it may be regarded as only the basis of a greater
industry. The present factory has accommodation to
produce from four to five thousand pairs of boots

Carlow once had a jail, and a very big jail it was.
Like all jails, it had a massive stone entrance. ven
the gallows is still to be seen. But jails are going out
of fashion in this mild and peaceful land.  Over the
gateway of the jail we now read ‘‘ Thompson and Son,
Ltd., Hanover Works.” Thompson’s, who started
about forty years ago, are chiefly and widely known as
makers of hay barns and constructive iron work. Even
to travellers by train, hay sheds are now as familiar a
sight as modern labourers’ cottages. Mr. Fred
Thompson remarked to us that every second farmer in
County Limerick had a hay shed. This Carlow firm,
we were informed, turns out about 250 hay sheds every
year, and at the moment they had men working in
sixteen counties in Ireland. The raw sheeting comes
from Antwerp, and all the rest is done by the firm,
the curving of the sheets and the making of the iron
frame work. The one time church of the one time jail
is now an office : the traces of the prison cells are quite
plain in the joiner’s shop as well as in the foundry.
Some 200 men, we were informed, are employed by
this Carlow firm, about half of whom are engaged in
the iron shed construction department. Quite recently
the firm opened a branch in Waterford, and have taken
the Neptune Iron Works. Steel and concrete work is
a special feature of the business of the firm, and they
have just taken a contract to build a margarine factory
in Waterford. s

There were Molloy’s Hosiery, Shackleton’s Mills,

Carlow ‘“at no far distant date.”’ Carlow boots are

As years went on the accuracy of my judgment
became clearer—to me at any rate.  Before long my
step-mother combined our several incomes 'mto a
common purse, and the anxieties and humiliations of
my position then became even more acute. Why, my
dear, will you believe it? I found myself quite a
cipher in my own house! We live, you know, side by
side, and semi-detached. I was powerlese even %o
regulate my expenditure according to my own tastes
and circumstances. She—if you please—appointed
my servants, most of them at wages I deemed alto-
gether too high. Positively robbing! She gave all the
orders.  She, in short, to use an American phrase, .
““hossed the show.’” Then, as you already know,
dear, we had great tiffs about religion. My step-
mother is Protestant, and insisted on treating me as
if I were the same! She even went so far as to appoint
chaplains to my household, but on this point at least T
was firm, and succeeded after an unseemly wrangle in
securing freedom of conscience.

Then, of course, there was all that bother about my
little bit of land. ' This my step-mother actually
bestowed on a number of idle and incompetent ne’er-
do-wells who did nothing to improve the place, and
who, when I claimed a right to it, actually had to be
bought-off at considerable expense. My step-mother
did, indeed, find “ the ready,”” as it is called for this
transaction (small thanks to her), but I have to pay her
back—with interest.

However, having so far succeeded as to secure my
religious freedom and the right to my bit of ground, T
picked up enough courage to renew my original stand
for at least some measure of authority in my own
house. On this point, you know, I had never really
given in, but my step-mother, as 1 have remarked, is
a very masterful person, and by this time has secured
such a hold over my cash and has so stocked my home
with her dependents that I am in a very difficult posi-
tion. A bright idea occurred to me, however. I began
to meddle in her affaivs! I interfered, whenever I got
the chance, in all her domestic concerns. I stirred up
dissension in her household. I crossed her whenever
possible in her out-o’-door concerns. This plan, com-
bined with the wearing effect of our continual and
bitter disputes about religion and the bit of land and
household affairs generally, made her ** sit-up,”” so to
speak. TFinally, when she made the disquieting dis-
covery (for she really is rather close) that she was
losing money over running my house, my hitherto 7
obdurate step-parent at last began to listen to my
repeated requests for a readjustment of our domestic
relations.

And it was high time, too, I may tell you. Of late
years the good lady has gone in for downright lavish
expendituve! She is running her household—and, of
course, mine as well—on a scale out of all proportion
to what I can afford. And not only that, but her tastes
and ideas are by no means the same as mine.
Naturally T am getting her into debt, or at least appar-
ently so, for she long ago spent what she took from me,
and as she does the household bills and accounts I am
far from clear as to how we really stand.

Well, we have discussed the terms of, the new
system.  They have been drafted, of course, by my
stern step-pavent. The separation is to extend only to
my purely domestic affairs. For the purpose of meet-
ing the outside world we are to act as one family, and

With regard to the outdoor servants, the proposed

That is, in a few words, my dear, the proposition
now before me. The proposals have yet to be ratified,

money. I can effect such economies or indulge in
such expenditure as I think fit. Surely this is some-
thing? Besides, dear, what better can I do?

On the other hand, many friends say I should be
jubilant, satisfied, and grateful. They say that this pro-
posed arrangement should make me positively love my
step-mother. Well, taking all things into considera-
tion, it doesn’t strike me as a matter for’love or
gratitude precisely—what do you think, denr?A

Mr. W. B. McCufcheon, seconding the resolution,
made o fine fighting speech. Here is just one extract: —
*“The fact of the matter is, people are too apt to meet,
discuss things, then go home and go to sleep; but the

Conference to ‘‘ draw the sword and throw away the
scabbard until they had made Victoria a place which
they could respect and feel proud of.”

Mr. McCutcheon’s speech was followed by some dis-
cussion with regard to the election of a committee, and
it was decided, eventually, that sixteen members be
selected by the delegates, and that ten more be chosen
by the elected sixteen. The following societies are
represented on the committee :—The Catholic Clergy,
the Anglican Social Questions Committee, the Austra-
lian Church, Social Reform Bureau, British Medical
Association, Council of Churches, Newsagents’ Asso-
ciation, Protestant Alliance, Hibernian-Australasian
Catholic Benefit Society, Methodist Conference, Race
Preservation League, Victorian Rationalist Association,
Australian Catholic Federation, Australian C.T.S., and
the Australian Literature Society.

The Rev. Dean Stephen announced that the Chapter
House of St. Paul’s Cathedral might be used as a
meeting-room for the newly-elected committee, and
this kind offer on the part of the Anglican authorities
was gratefully accepted. °

In concluding this brief sketch of the Melbourne
Conference, may I ask: Could not a similar conference
be convened in Belfast, and in many other Irish towns,
to which this very urgent movement has not yet
extended ?

I am not entitled to speak for anyone in this matter
except for myself, still I think I only interpret the feel-
ings of many in Ireland in saying that they are most
grateful to Australia for the encouragement, and the
splendid example which she has given to the workers
in a like noble cause in the old countries.

AN “IRISHMAN.”

REMEMBER the day as if it were only yesterday,
though more than eight years have gone by since
then. We had come to school that morning with much
the same feelings that school-boys in general become
possessed of when the summer sun is shining in a cloud-
less sky and the birds are singing gaily in the trees—a
morning when you felt glad to be alive, and when you
appreciated to some extent the greatest of God’s gifts—
Liberty ; when your heart overflowed with pity for the
poor wretches cooped up in prisons and workhouses
and—though civilisation is supposed to be rife amongst
us—in stuffy, overcrowded, disease-laden National
schools. Monitor though I was, I came to school very

AN OLD PLAY AT BLACKROCK,

WENT to ‘‘Arrah na Pogue’’ (Ara na Poige) the other
night for the first time. In view of the fact that
the play is very popular, some first impressions may
prove interesting, the more so, as it has come to be
fashionable to decry Boucicault and his plays among
certain literary circles in much the same way as Moore
and his poor half-strung harp, pikes, tin and otherwise,
rebellion, lip and otherwise, have been relegated to the
lumber end of our National property room, as beneath
the dignity of 20th century Ireland. We are all but
actors, Ta’'s agat, and look at one of these time-worn
efforts of our immediate ancestors might improve the
modern acting or show us that we have progressed in
the change.

Most things old are venerable, so bear with me, dear
reader.

The function of the play is, I believe, to instruct and
amuse. .

Ara na Poige teaches many lessons; the hatred for
an informer, for instance, that is to say, for anything
sneaking and dishonourable, and our audience hoswled
with delight when Feeney was thrown over the cliff.
The ignorance of the average Englishman about Irish
customs is shown cleverly in the mistake of an officer
in correcting Shawn na Post for calling a brother officer
““ O’Grady,” his title as an Irish chieftain.

This same O'Grady teaches the lesson of the unfor-
tunate Gael who in order to give rein to his free and
independent spirit, elsewhere than at Cremona and
Fontenoy, turns to the alien faith (as did Antony
Malene, the precursor of Grattan, for example), bub
never feels quite at home among the time servers with
whom his perversion compels him to herd. He wishes
to save his clansman (Shawn na Post) at courtmartial,
whose attempt to remain loyal to his perverted chief
and to his country ab the same time, lands him into the

Then we have as a contrast to the ‘“ loyal ’ chieftain
O’Grady, the rebel chieftain, The McCoul, and their
different fates, each courting the same lady. The one
remains secure at home, by a noble sacrifice on this

hand of the lady.

The singing of ‘‘ The Wearing of the Green’ at the
wedding party, as the guests keep a sharp lookout for
the soldiery, the omniscience of his Majesty’s Govern-
ment, which prys into the secrets of all parties, so
amusingly burlesqued, contributes to the scheme of
instruction. ‘‘ Shawn (Seaghan) is the Irish for John,”
said the courtmartial. *‘ No, but John is the English
for Shawn,’’ said the redoubtable post driver, and we
all cheered.

There is one bad lesson, which has been too well
learnt, alas! and that is the sorrowful leave-taking by
MecCoul of his native land. Very dramatic, I am sure,
and a lot of people think it a very dramatic, heroic
thing to leave their own country in this ** tender melan-
choly ”’ style, just as Mr. William O’Brien thinks it
heroie to retire from public life occasionally.

The ““ heroism *’ of suicide is not more foolish and
dishonourable than this heroism of the emigrant. An
Irish man and woman should leave their country with
shame, that they have not the pluck and grit to face
the battle of life at home.

Speaking generally, here is the emigrant problem ; he
who leaves his country is the village hero; he who
stays, the family drudge or the hampered office holder.

There is one other bad lessom, the slavish respect of
the clansmen (the modern ‘‘ peasantry *’) to their chiefs.

There is plenty of amusement. Feeney, the infor-
mer, whose inside is like a ‘“ bunch of fiddlestrings "’
with fright, and his * collogings with the devil ” would
male a cat laugh.

To sum up, whether the people taught Boucicault
or Boucicault taught the people, they knew their lesson
or learnt their lesson well. I hope the same thing will
come of our modern dramatists, and that their lessons
will be good.

An Irish company, the St. John’s Dramatic Society,
Blaclkrock, produced the play for us. I may be pre-
judiced, but I think they were remarkably good. The
self-possession yet constraint of such young actors was
astonishing, and there was no affectation. The elocu-
tion was excellent, and while they never imitdted the
“‘Abbey’’ school, they had not the jurnpy uncertainties
of English acting. I humbly express the hope that
they will try some modern dramas in the next year’s
work Mr. Devitt told us of.
